question:
Let $\Gamma$ consist of all polynomials in $x$ with integer
coefficients. For $f$ and $g$ in $\Gamma$ and $m$ a positive integer,
let $f \equiv g \pmod{m}$ mean that every coefficient of $f-g$ is an
integral multiple of $m$. Let $n$ and $p$ be positive integers with
$p$ prime. Given that $f,g,h,r$ and $s$ are in $\Gamma$ with
$rf+sg\equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ and $fg \equiv h \pmod{p}$, prove that there
exist $F$ and $G$ in $\Gamma$ with $F \equiv f \pmod{p}$, $G \equiv g
\pmod{p}$, and $FG \equiv h \pmod{p^n}$.
solution:
Solution. We prove by induction on \( k \) that there exist polynomials \( F_{k}, G_{k} \in \Gamma \) such that \( F_{k} \equiv f(\bmod p), G_{k} \equiv g(\bmod p) \), and \( F_{k} G_{k} \equiv h\left(\bmod p^{k}\right) \). For the base case \( k=1 \), we take \( F_{1}=f, G_{1}=g \).

For the inductive step, we assume the existence \( F_{k}, G_{k} \) as above, and try to construct \( F_{k+1}, G_{k+1} \). By assumption, \( h-F_{k} G_{k}=p^{k} t \), for some \( t \in \Gamma \). We will \( \operatorname{try} F_{k+1}=F_{k}+p^{k} \Delta_{1} \) and \( G_{k+1}=G_{k}+p^{k} \Delta_{2} \), where \( \Delta_{1}, \Delta_{2} \in \Gamma \) are yet to be chosen. Then \( F_{k+1} \equiv F_{k} \equiv f(\bmod p), G_{k+1} \equiv G_{k} \equiv g(\bmod p) \), and
\[
\begin{aligned}
F_{k+1} G_{k+1} & =F_{k} G_{k}+p^{k}\left(\Delta_{2} F_{k}+\Delta_{1} G_{k}\right)+p^{2 k} \Delta_{1} \Delta_{2} \\
& \equiv F_{k} G_{k}+p^{k}\left(\Delta_{2} F_{k}+\Delta_{1} G_{k}\right) \quad\left(\bmod p^{k+1}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

If we choose \( \Delta_{2}=t r \) and \( \Delta_{1}=t s \), then
\[
\Delta_{2} F_{k}+\Delta_{1} G_{k} \equiv \operatorname{tr} f+t s g=t(r f+s g) \equiv t \quad(\bmod p)
\]
so \( p^{k}\left(\Delta_{2} F_{k}+\Delta_{1} G_{k}\right) \equiv p^{k} t\left(\bmod p^{k+1}\right) \), and \( F_{k+1} G_{k+1} \equiv F_{k} G_{k}+p^{k} t=h\left(\bmod p^{k+1}\right) \), completing the inductive step.

Remark. This problem is a special case of a version of Hensel's Lemma [Ei, p. 208], a fundamental result in number theory. Here is a different version [NZM, Theorem 2.23], which can be thought of as a \( p \)-adic analogue of Newton's method for solving polynomial equations via successive approximation:

Hensel's Lemma. Suppose that \( f(x) \) is a polynomial with integral coefficients. If \( f(a) \equiv 0\left(\bmod p^{j}\right) \) and \( f^{\prime}(a) \not \equiv 0(\bmod p) \), then there is a unique \( t(\bmod p) \) such that \( f\left(a+t p^{j}\right) \equiv 0\left(\bmod p^{j+1}\right) \).

descriptive_long.question: Let $polyset$ consist of all polynomials in $variablex$ with integer coefficients. For $polynomf$ and $polynomg$ in $polyset$ and $modulusm$ a positive integer, let $polynomf \equiv polynomg \pmod{modulusm}$ mean that every coefficient of $polynomf-polynomg$ is an integral multiple of $modulusm$. Let $exponentn$ and $primep$ be positive integers with $primep$ prime. Given that $polynomf,polynomg,polynomh,scalarer$ and $scalars$ are in $polyset$ with $scalarer polynomf+scalars polynomg\equiv 1 \pmod{primep}$ and $polynomf polynomg \equiv polynomh \pmod{primep}$, prove that there exist $adjustedf$ and $adjustedg$ in $polyset$ with $adjustedf \equiv polynomf \pmod{primep}$, $adjustedg \equiv polynomg \pmod{primep}$, and $adjustedf adjustedg \equiv polynomh \pmod{primep^{exponentn}}$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. We prove by induction on \( indexk \) that there exist polynomials \( sequencef, sequenceg \in polyset \) such that \( sequencef \equiv polynomf(\bmod primep), sequenceg \equiv polynomg(\bmod primep) \), and \( sequencef sequenceg \equiv polynomh\left(\bmod primep^{indexk}\right) \). For the base case \( indexk=1 \), we take \( sequencef=polynomf, sequenceg=polynomg \).

For the inductive step, we assume the existence \( sequencef, sequenceg \) as above, and try to construct \( sequencef, sequenceg \). By assumption, \( polynomh-sequencef sequenceg=primep^{indexk} polyt \), for some \( polyt \in polyset \). We will \operatorname{try} sequencef=sequencef+primep^{indexk} deltaone and sequenceg=sequenceg+primep^{indexk} deltatwo, where \( deltaone, deltatwo \in polyset \) are yet to be chosen. Then \( sequencef \equiv sequencef \equiv polynomf(\bmod primep), sequenceg \equiv sequenceg \equiv polynomg(\bmod primep) \), and
\[
\begin{aligned}
sequencef sequenceg & =sequencef sequenceg+primep^{indexk}\left(deltatwo sequencef+deltaone sequenceg\right)+primep^{2 indexk} deltaone deltatwo \\
& \equiv sequencef sequenceg+primep^{indexk}\left(deltatwo sequencef+deltaone sequenceg\right) \quad\left(\bmod primep^{indexk+1}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

If we choose \( deltatwo=polyt scalarer \) and \( deltaone=polyt scalars \), then
\[
deltatwo sequencef+deltaone sequenceg \equiv polyt scalarer polynomf+polyt scalars polynomg=polyt( scalarer polynomf+scalars polynomg ) \equiv polyt \quad(\bmod primep)
\]
so \( primep^{indexk}\left(deltatwo sequencef+deltaone sequenceg\right) \equiv primep^{indexk} polyt\left(\bmod primep^{indexk+1}\right) \), and \( sequencef sequenceg \equiv sequencef sequenceg+primep^{indexk} polyt=polynomh\left(\bmod primep^{indexk+1}\right) \), completing the inductive step.

Remark. This problem is a special case of a version of Hensel's Lemma [Ei, primep. 208], a fundamental result in number theory. Here is a different version [NZM, Theorem 2.23], which can be thought of as a \( primep \)-adic analogue of Newton's method for solving polynomial equations via successive approximation:

Hensel's Lemma. Suppose that \( polynomf(variablex) \) is a polynomial with integral coefficients. If \( polynomf(pointa) \equiv 0\left(\bmod primep^{indexj}\right) \) and \( polynomf^{\prime}(pointa) \not \equiv 0(\bmod primep) \), then there is a unique \( polyt(\bmod primep) \) such that \( polynomf\left(pointa+polyt primep^{indexj}\right) \equiv 0\left(\bmod primep^{indexj+1}\right) \).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $\tapestry$ consist of all polynomials in $dandelion$ with integer
coefficients. For $\melodyline$ and $\breadcrumb$ in $\tapestry$ and
$\cyanotype$ a positive integer, let $\melodyline \equiv \breadcrumb \pmod{\cyanotype}$ mean that every coefficient of $\melodyline-\breadcrumb$ is an
integral multiple of $\cyanotype$. Let $\brickhouse$ and $\drumstick$ be positive integers with
$\drumstick$ prime. Given that $\melodyline,\breadcrumb,\starlight,\compassio$ and $\daydream$ are in $\tapestry$ with
$\compassio\melodyline+\daydream\breadcrumb\equiv 1 \pmod{\drumstick}$ and $\melodyline\breadcrumb \equiv \starlight \pmod{\drumstick}$, prove that there
exist $\nightshade$ and $\afterglow$ in $\tapestry$ with $\nightshade \equiv \melodyline \pmod{\drumstick}$, $\afterglow \equiv \breadcrumb
\pmod{\drumstick}$, and $\nightshade\afterglow \equiv \starlight \pmod{\drumstick^{\brickhouse}}$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. We prove by induction on $ \sugarcoat $ that there exist polynomials $ 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}, \afterglow_{\sugarcoat} \in 	apestry $ such that $ 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat} \equiv \melodyline( mod \drumstick),\ \afterglow_{\sugarcoat} \equiv  readcrumb( mod \drumstick) $, and $ 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}\afterglow_{\sugarcoat} \equiv \starlight\left( mod \drumstick^{\sugarcoat}ight) $. For the base case $ \sugarcoat=1 $, we take $ 
ightshade_{1}=\melodyline, \afterglow_{1}= readcrumb $.

For the inductive step, we assume the existence of $ 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}, \afterglow_{\sugarcoat} $ as above, and try to construct $ 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat+1}, \afterglow_{\sugarcoat+1} $. By assumption, $ \starlight-
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}\afterglow_{\sugarcoat}=\drumstick^{\sugarcoat} \quasarbeam $, for some $ \quasarbeam \in 	apestry $. We will try $ 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat+1}=
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}+\drumstick^{\sugarcoat} \peregrine $ and $ \afterglow_{\sugarcoat+1}=\afterglow_{\sugarcoat}+\drumstick^{\sugarcoat} \wildfire $, where $ \peregrine, \wildfire \in 	apestry $ are yet to be chosen. Then $ 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat+1} \equiv 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat} \equiv \melodyline( mod \drumstick),\ \afterglow_{\sugarcoat+1} \equiv \afterglow_{\sugarcoat} \equiv  readcrumb( mod \drumstick) $, and
\[
 egin{aligned}

ightshade_{\sugarcoat+1}\afterglow_{\sugarcoat+1} & =
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}\afterglow_{\sugarcoat}+\drumstick^{\sugarcoat}\left(\wildfire 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}+\peregrine \afterglow_{\sugarcoat}ight)+\drumstick^{2 \sugarcoat} \peregrine \wildfire \
& \equiv 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}\afterglow_{\sugarcoat}+\drumstick^{\sugarcoat}\left(\wildfire 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}+\peregrine \afterglow_{\sugarcoat}ight) \quad\left( mod \drumstick^{\sugarcoat+1}ight)
\end{aligned}
\]

If we choose $ \wildfire=\quasarbeam \compassio $ and $ \peregrine=\quasarbeam \daydream $, then
\[
\wildfire 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}+\peregrine \afterglow_{\sugarcoat} \equiv \quasarbeam \compassio \melodyline+\quasarbeam \daydream  readcrumb=\quasarbeam(\compassio \melodyline+\daydream  readcrumb) \equiv \quasarbeam \quad( mod \drumstick)
\]
so $ \drumstick^{\sugarcoat}\left(\wildfire 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}+\peregrine \afterglow_{\sugarcoat}ight) \equiv \drumstick^{\sugarcoat} \quasarbeam\left( mod \drumstick^{\sugarcoat+1}ight) $, and $ 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat+1}\afterglow_{\sugarcoat+1} \equiv 
ightshade_{\sugarcoat}\afterglow_{\sugarcoat}+\drumstick^{\sugarcoat} \quasarbeam=\starlight\left( mod \drumstick^{\sugarcoat+1}ight) $, completing the inductive step.

Remark. This problem is a special case of a version of Hensel's Lemma [Ei, p. 208], a fundamental result in number theory. Here is a different version [NZM, Theorem 2.23], which can be thought of as a $ \drumstick $-adic analogue of Newton's method for solving polynomial equations via successive approximation:

Hensel's Lemma. Suppose that $ \melodyline(dandelion) $ is a polynomial with integral coefficients. If $ \melodyline(\lighthouse) \equiv 0\left( mod \drumstick^{\windchime}ight) $ and $ \melodyline^{\prime}(\lighthouse) 
ot \equiv 0( mod \drumstick) $, then there is a unique $ \quasarbeam( mod \drumstick) $ such that $ \melodyline\left(\lighthouse+\quasarbeam \drumstick^{\windchime}ight) \equiv 0\left( mod \drumstick^{\windchime+1}ight) $.

descriptive_long_misleading.question: Let $\antialgebra$ consist of all polynomials in $invariable$ with integer coefficients. For $malfunction$ and $inertpoly$ in $\antialgebra$ and $numerator$ a positive integer, let $malfunction \equiv inertpoly \pmod{numerator}$ mean that every coefficient of $malfunction-inertpoly$ is an integral multiple of $numerator$. Let $mantissa$ and $composite$ be positive integers with $composite$ prime. Given that $malfunction,inertpoly,quotient,remainder$ and $dividend$ are in $\antialgebra$ with $remainder malfunction+dividend inertpoly\equiv 1 \pmod{composite}$ and $malfunction inertpoly \equiv quotient \pmod{composite}$, prove that there exist $shallower$ and $regressor$ in $\antialgebra$ with $shallower \equiv malfunction \pmod{composite}$, $regressor \equiv inertpoly \pmod{composite}$, and $shallower regressor \equiv quotient \pmod{composite^{mantissa}}$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. We prove by induction on \( terminal \) that there exist polynomials \( shallower_{terminal}, regressor_{terminal} \in \antialgebra \) such that \( shallower_{terminal} \equiv malfunction(\bmod composite), regressor_{terminal} \equiv inertpoly(\bmod composite) \), and \( shallower_{terminal} regressor_{terminal} \equiv quotient\left(\bmod composite^{terminal}\right) \). For the base case \( terminal=1 \), we take \( shallower_{1}=malfunction, regressor_{1}=inertpoly \).

For the inductive step, we assume the existence \( shallower_{terminal}, regressor_{terminal} \) as above, and try to construct \( shallower_{terminal+1}, regressor_{terminal+1} \). By assumption, \( quotient-shallower_{terminal} regressor_{terminal}=composite^{terminal} constant \), for some \( constant \in \antialgebra \). We will \operatorname{try} \( shallower_{terminal+1}=shallower_{terminal}+composite^{terminal} \steadiness_{1} \) and \( regressor_{terminal+1}=regressor_{terminal}+composite^{terminal} \uniformity_{2} \), where \( \steadiness_{1}, \uniformity_{2} \in \antialgebra \) are yet to be chosen. Then \( shallower_{terminal+1} \equiv shallower_{terminal} \equiv malfunction(\bmod composite), regressor_{terminal+1} \equiv regressor_{terminal} \equiv inertpoly(\bmod composite) \), and
\[
\begin{aligned}
shallower_{terminal+1} regressor_{terminal+1} & =shallower_{terminal} regressor_{terminal}+composite^{terminal}\left(\uniformity_{2} shallower_{terminal}+\steadiness_{1} regressor_{terminal}\right)+composite^{2 terminal} \steadiness_{1} \uniformity_{2} \\
& \equiv shallower_{terminal} regressor_{terminal}+composite^{terminal}\left(\uniformity_{2} shallower_{terminal}+\steadiness_{1} regressor_{terminal}\right) \quad\left(\bmod composite^{terminal+1}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

If we choose \( \uniformity_{2}=constant\, remainder \) and \( \steadiness_{1}=constant\, dividend \), then
\[
\uniformity_{2} shallower_{terminal}+\steadiness_{1} regressor_{terminal} \equiv \operatorname{constant\,remainder} malfunction+constant dividend inertpoly=constant(remainder malfunction+dividend inertpoly) \equiv constant \quad(\bmod composite)
\]
so \( composite^{terminal}\left(\uniformity_{2} shallower_{terminal}+\steadiness_{1} regressor_{terminal}\right) \equiv composite^{terminal} constant\left(\bmod composite^{terminal+1}\right) \), and \( shallower_{terminal+1} regressor_{terminal+1} \equiv shallower_{terminal} regressor_{terminal}+composite^{terminal} constant=quotient\left(\bmod composite^{terminal+1}\right) \), completing the inductive step.

Remark. This problem is a special case of a version of Hensel's Lemma [Ei, p. 208], a fundamental result in number theory. Here is a different version [NZM, Theorem 2.23], which can be thought of as a \( composite \)-adic analogue of Newton's method for solving polynomial equations via successive approximation:

Hensel's Lemma. Suppose that \( malfunction(invariable) \) is a polynomial with integral coefficients. If \( malfunction(nonfactor) \equiv 0\left(\bmod composite^{baseline}\right) \) and \( malfunction^{\prime}(nonfactor) \not \equiv 0(\bmod composite) \), then there is a unique \( constant(\bmod composite) \) such that \( malfunction\left(nonfactor+constant\, composite^{baseline}\right) \equiv 0\left(\bmod composite^{baseline+1}\right) \).

garbled_string.question:
Let $gstbrpqe$ consist of all polynomials in $qzxwvtnp$ with integer
coefficients. For $hjgrksla$ and $mvdqplko$ in $gstbrpqe$ and $yzlkrqwa$ a positive integer,
let $hjgrksla \equiv mvdqplko \pmod{yzlkrqwa}$ mean that every coefficient of $hjgrksla-mvdqplko$ is an
integral multiple of $yzlkrqwa$. Let $fxvtmeoj$ and $sbhuqane$ be positive integers with
$sbhuqane$ prime. Given that $hjgrksla,mvdqplko,tczbrysu,oiwemcfa$ and $blyxdrku$ are in $gstbrpqe$ with
$oiwemcfa hjgrksla+blyxdrku mvdqplko\equiv 1 \pmod{sbhuqane}$ and $hjgrksla mvdqplko \equiv tczbrysu \pmod{sbhuqane}$, prove that there
exist $wneurxza$ and $kvodimce$ in $gstbrpqe$ with $wneurxza \equiv hjgrksla \pmod{sbhuqane}$, $kvodimce \equiv mvdqplko
\pmod{sbhuqane}$, and $wneurxza kvodimce \equiv tczbrysu \pmod{sbhuqane^{fxvtmeoj}}$.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. We prove by induction on \( vpanqjoi \) that there exist polynomials \( wneurxza_{vpanqjoi}, kvodimce_{vpanqjoi} \in gstbrpqe \) such that \( wneurxza_{vpanqjoi} \equiv hjgrksla(\bmod sbhuqane),\; kvodimce_{vpanqjoi} \equiv mvdqplko(\bmod sbhuqane) \), and \( wneurxza_{vpanqjoi} kvodimce_{vpanqjoi} \equiv tczbrysu\left(\bmod sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi}\right) \). For the base case \( vpanqjoi=1 \), we take \( wneurxza_{1}=hjgrksla,\; kvodimce_{1}=mvdqplko \).

For the inductive step, we assume the existence \( wneurxza_{vpanqjoi}, kvodimce_{vpanqjoi} \) as above, and try to construct \( wneurxza_{vpanqjoi+1}, kvodimce_{vpanqjoi+1} \). By assumption,
\( tczbrysu-wneurxza_{vpanqjoi} kvodimce_{vpanqjoi}=sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi} lfgscmbr \),
for some \( lfgscmbr \in gstbrpqe \). We will \( \operatorname{try} \;
wneurxza_{vpanqjoi+1}=wneurxza_{vpanqjoi}+sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi} nvxqlokr \) and
\( kvodimce_{vpanqjoi+1}=kvodimce_{vpanqjoi}+sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi} rpzetmsh \), where
\( nvxqlokr, rpzetmsh \in gstbrpqe \) are yet to be chosen. Then
\( wneurxza_{vpanqjoi+1} \equiv wneurxza_{vpanqjoi} \equiv hjgrksla(\bmod sbhuqane),\;
kvodimce_{vpanqjoi+1} \equiv kvodimce_{vpanqjoi} \equiv mvdqplko(\bmod sbhuqane) \), and
\[
\begin{aligned}
wneurxza_{vpanqjoi+1} kvodimce_{vpanqjoi+1}
& =wneurxza_{vpanqjoi} kvodimce_{vpanqjoi}
 +sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi}\left(rpzetmsh wneurxza_{vpanqjoi}+nvxqlokr kvodimce_{vpanqjoi}\right)
 +sbhuqane^{2 vpanqjoi} nvxqlokr rpzetmsh \\
& \equiv wneurxza_{vpanqjoi} kvodimce_{vpanqjoi}
 +sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi}\left(rpzetmsh wneurxza_{vpanqjoi}+nvxqlokr kvodimce_{vpanqjoi}\right)
 \quad\left(\bmod sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi+1}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

If we choose \( rpzetmsh=lfgscmbr oiwemcfa \) and \( nvxqlokr=lfgscmbr blyxdrku \), then
\[
rpzetmsh wneurxza_{vpanqjoi}+nvxqlokr kvodimce_{vpanqjoi}
\equiv \operatorname{tr} hjgrksla+lfgscmbr blyxdrku mvdqplko
=lfgscmbr(oiwemcfa hjgrksla+blyxdrku mvdqplko)
\equiv lfgscmbr \quad(\bmod sbhuqane)
\]
so \( sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi}\left(rpzetmsh wneurxza_{vpanqjoi}+nvxqlokr kvodimce_{vpanqjoi}\right)
\equiv sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi} lfgscmbr\left(\bmod sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi+1}\right) \), and
\( wneurxza_{vpanqjoi+1} kvodimce_{vpanqjoi+1} \equiv wneurxza_{vpanqjoi} kvodimce_{vpanqjoi}
+sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi} lfgscmbr = tczbrysu\left(\bmod sbhuqane^{vpanqjoi+1}\right) \), completing the inductive step.

Remark. This problem is a special case of a version of Hensel's Lemma [Ei, p. 208], a fundamental result in number theory. Here is a different version [NZM, Theorem 2.23], which can be thought of as a \( sbhuqane \)-adic analogue of Newton's method for solving polynomial equations via successive approximation:

Hensel's Lemma. Suppose that \( hjgrksla(qzxwvtnp) \) is a polynomial with integral coefficients. If \( hjgrksla(dacirnwe) \equiv 0\left(\bmod sbhuqane^{dfkgzwlm}\right) \) and \( hjgrksla^{\prime}(dacirnwe) \not \equiv 0(\bmod sbhuqane) \), then there is a unique \( lfgscmbr(\bmod sbhuqane) \) such that \( hjgrksla\left(dacirnwe+lfgscmbr sbhuqane^{dfkgzwlm}\right) \equiv 0\left(\bmod sbhuqane^{dfkgzwlm+1}\right) \).

kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer d \geq  1 and put \Gamma  := \mathbb{Z}[x_1,\ldots ,x_d].  
For u,v \in  \Gamma  and an integer M \geq  2 we write  

  u \equiv  v (mod M)  

when every coefficient of u-v is divisible by M.

Let p be an odd prime, let \ell  \geq  1 and set N := p^\ell .  
Suppose that polynomials  

  a , b , h , r , s \in  \Gamma                                                    (*)

satisfy  

(1)  r a + s b \equiv  1 (mod p)                     (Bezout relation)  
(2)  a b        \equiv  h (mod p)                     (product condition)  
(3)  r a - s b \equiv  c (mod p) with c \in  (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})x; i.e. the reduction of r a-s b
     is a non-zero constant modulo p (non-degeneracy).

Prove that there exist polynomials A,B \in  \Gamma  such that  

(i) A \equiv  a (mod p) and B \equiv  b (mod p);  
(ii) r A + s B \equiv  1 (mod N);  
(iii) A B    \equiv  h (mod N).

In particular, under the additional unit condition (3) the Bezout
identity and the prescribed value of the product can be lifted
simultaneously from modulus p to modulus p^\ell .
kernel_variant.solution:
We imitate a two-variable Hensel lifting, the extra hypothesis (3)
ensuring an invertible Jacobian.

Write K := F_p[x_1,\ldots ,x_d] and denote reduction modulo p with a bar.
For k = 1,2,\ldots ,\ell  define the statement  

 P_k : ``there exist A_k , B_k \in  \Gamma  such that  
 (a) A_k \equiv  a (mod p), B_k \equiv  b (mod p);  
 (b) r A_k + s B_k \equiv  1 (mod p^{\,k});  
 (c) A_k B_k        \equiv  h (mod p^{\,k}).''

Our goal is to build A_\ell ,B_\ell ; we then set A:=A_\ell , B:=B_\ell .

Base step k = 1.  
Take A_1 := a, B_1 := b.  By (1)-(2) the three conditions hold.

Induction step.  
Assume 1 \leq  k < \ell  and that A_k,B_k meet P_k.  Write the
``product'' and ``Bezout'' errors:

 h - A_k B_k = p^{\,k} t_k  (t_k \in  \Gamma ),                     (3')  
 1 -(r A_k+s B_k)= p^{\,k} v_k  (v_k \in  \Gamma ).              (4')

Seek corrections \Delta _A,\Delta _B \in  \Gamma  and put  

 A_{k+1}:=A_k+p^{\,k}\Delta _A, B_{k+1}:=B_k+p^{\,k}\Delta _B.        (5')

(i) Trivially A_{k+1}\equiv a and B_{k+1}\equiv b (mod p).

(ii) Bezout combination modulo p^{k+1}:

 r A_{k+1}+s B_{k+1}  
 \equiv  r A_k+s B_k + p^{\,k}(r \Delta _A+s \Delta _B)  
 \equiv  1 - p^{\,k}v_k + p^{\,k}(r \Delta _A+s \Delta _B).         (by (4'))

Requiring r \Delta _A+s \Delta _B \equiv  v_k (mod p) guarantees
r A_{k+1}+s B_{k+1} \equiv  1 (mod p^{k+1}).                (6')

(iii) Product modulo p^{k+1}:

 A_{k+1}B_{k+1}  
 \equiv  A_k B_k + p^{\,k}(\Delta _A B_k+A_k \Delta _B)             (mod p^{k+1}).  
Using (3') we need

 \Delta _A B_k + A_k \Delta _B \equiv  t_k (mod p).                       (7')

Hence we must solve the linear system over K

  [ r s ] [\Delta _A] = [v_k]  
  [ B_k A_k ] [\Delta _B]   [t_k]                            (8')

The determinant is

 det M_k = r A_k - s B_k.                           (9')

Modulo p we have A_k\equiv a, B_k\equiv b, so

 det M_k \equiv  r a - s b = c \in  F_p^\times                     (10')

by the hypothesis (3).  Therefore det M_k is the non-zero constant c
and is a unit of the polynomial ring K, so M_k is invertible over K.
Consequently (8') admits a unique solution (\Delta _A,\Delta _B) in K^2.  Choose
arbitrary lifts \Delta _A,\Delta _B \in  \Gamma  of those residue-class solutions and
define A_{k+1},B_{k+1} by (5').  Then (6') and (7') are satisfied, so
P_{k+1} holds.

Completion.  
Induction produces A_\ell ,B_\ell  with P_\ell .  Setting A:=A_\ell  and B:=B_\ell  we
obtain

 A \equiv  a, B \equiv  b (mod p)                (by (a)),  
 rA+sB \equiv  1 (mod p^\ell )                (by (b)),  
 A B \equiv  h (mod p^\ell )                  (by (c)),

which completes the proof.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher-dimensional setting.  
   ŌĆó The problem works in the multivariate polynomial ring ╬ō = Ōäż[xŌéü,ŌĆ”,x_d] with dŌēź1,  
     rather than the univariate ring of the original statement.  

2. Non-commutative objects.  
   ŌĆó We pass from single polynomials to m├Śm polynomial *matrices*.  
     All computations must therefore respect non-commutativity, and right/left multiplications
     have to be distinguished throughout.

3. Multiple simultaneous constraints.  
   ŌĆó We are asked to lift *three* identities at once: a B├®zout combination,
     equality of the two products AB and BA with a prescribed matrix H, and
     commutativity of the pair (A,B).  
   ŌĆó Each extra constraint introduces an additional linear condition that has
     to be preserved at every induction step.

4. Surjectivity in a simple Artinian ring.  
   ŌĆó Ensuring solvability of the linear system for the corrections
     (╬ö_A,╬ö_B) can no longer rely on elementary gcd arguments (which make sense only
     in commutative rings).  One needs the structure theory of full matrix
     rings, in particular their simplicity, to prove that a certain ╬ø╠ä-linear
     map is surjective.

5. Depth of induction.  
   ŌĆó The proof is an elaborate matrix-valued version of HenselŌĆÖs lemma,
     carried out simultaneously for several conditions and inside a
     non-commutative ring.  Each induction step requires working with three
     coupled matrix equations, instead of one scalar equation in the original
     Olympiad problem.

These layers of extra dimensions, non-commutativity, and interacting
constraints make the enhanced variant substantially harder than both the
initial question and the current kernel version.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Fix an integer d \geq  1 and put \Gamma  := \mathbb{Z}[x_1,\ldots ,x_d].  
For u,v \in  \Gamma  and an integer M \geq  2 we write  

  u \equiv  v (mod M)  

when every coefficient of u-v is divisible by M.

Let p be an odd prime, let \ell  \geq  1 and set N := p^\ell .  
Suppose that polynomials  

  a , b , h , r , s \in  \Gamma                                                    (*)

satisfy  

(1)  r a + s b \equiv  1 (mod p)                     (Bezout relation)  
(2)  a b        \equiv  h (mod p)                     (product condition)  
(3)  r a - s b \equiv  c (mod p) with c \in  (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})x; i.e. the reduction of r a-s b
     is a non-zero constant modulo p (non-degeneracy).

Prove that there exist polynomials A,B \in  \Gamma  such that  

(i) A \equiv  a (mod p) and B \equiv  b (mod p);  
(ii) r A + s B \equiv  1 (mod N);  
(iii) A B    \equiv  h (mod N).

In particular, under the additional unit condition (3) the Bezout
identity and the prescribed value of the product can be lifted
simultaneously from modulus p to modulus p^\ell .
original_kernel_variant.solution:
We imitate a two-variable Hensel lifting, the extra hypothesis (3)
ensuring an invertible Jacobian.

Write K := F_p[x_1,\ldots ,x_d] and denote reduction modulo p with a bar.
For k = 1,2,\ldots ,\ell  define the statement  

 P_k : ``there exist A_k , B_k \in  \Gamma  such that  
 (a) A_k \equiv  a (mod p), B_k \equiv  b (mod p);  
 (b) r A_k + s B_k \equiv  1 (mod p^{\,k});  
 (c) A_k B_k        \equiv  h (mod p^{\,k}).''

Our goal is to build A_\ell ,B_\ell ; we then set A:=A_\ell , B:=B_\ell .

Base step k = 1.  
Take A_1 := a, B_1 := b.  By (1)-(2) the three conditions hold.

Induction step.  
Assume 1 \leq  k < \ell  and that A_k,B_k meet P_k.  Write the
``product'' and ``Bezout'' errors:

 h - A_k B_k = p^{\,k} t_k  (t_k \in  \Gamma ),                     (3')  
 1 -(r A_k+s B_k)= p^{\,k} v_k  (v_k \in  \Gamma ).              (4')

Seek corrections \Delta _A,\Delta _B \in  \Gamma  and put  

 A_{k+1}:=A_k+p^{\,k}\Delta _A, B_{k+1}:=B_k+p^{\,k}\Delta _B.        (5')

(i) Trivially A_{k+1}\equiv a and B_{k+1}\equiv b (mod p).

(ii) Bezout combination modulo p^{k+1}:

 r A_{k+1}+s B_{k+1}  
 \equiv  r A_k+s B_k + p^{\,k}(r \Delta _A+s \Delta _B)  
 \equiv  1 - p^{\,k}v_k + p^{\,k}(r \Delta _A+s \Delta _B).         (by (4'))

Requiring r \Delta _A+s \Delta _B \equiv  v_k (mod p) guarantees
r A_{k+1}+s B_{k+1} \equiv  1 (mod p^{k+1}).                (6')

(iii) Product modulo p^{k+1}:

 A_{k+1}B_{k+1}  
 \equiv  A_k B_k + p^{\,k}(\Delta _A B_k+A_k \Delta _B)             (mod p^{k+1}).  
Using (3') we need

 \Delta _A B_k + A_k \Delta _B \equiv  t_k (mod p).                       (7')

Hence we must solve the linear system over K

  [ r s ] [\Delta _A] = [v_k]  
  [ B_k A_k ] [\Delta _B]   [t_k]                            (8')

The determinant is

 det M_k = r A_k - s B_k.                           (9')

Modulo p we have A_k\equiv a, B_k\equiv b, so

 det M_k \equiv  r a - s b = c \in  F_p^\times                     (10')

by the hypothesis (3).  Therefore det M_k is the non-zero constant c
and is a unit of the polynomial ring K, so M_k is invertible over K.
Consequently (8') admits a unique solution (\Delta _A,\Delta _B) in K^2.  Choose
arbitrary lifts \Delta _A,\Delta _B \in  \Gamma  of those residue-class solutions and
define A_{k+1},B_{k+1} by (5').  Then (6') and (7') are satisfied, so
P_{k+1} holds.

Completion.  
Induction produces A_\ell ,B_\ell  with P_\ell .  Setting A:=A_\ell  and B:=B_\ell  we
obtain

 A \equiv  a, B \equiv  b (mod p)                (by (a)),  
 rA+sB \equiv  1 (mod p^\ell )                (by (b)),  
 A B \equiv  h (mod p^\ell )                  (by (c)),

which completes the proof.
